# Ready-Bug Folder

**Purpose:** Auto-generated, copy-paste-ready bug reports. Each `.md` file in this folder represents one test failure and is formatted to be pasted directly into a Jira ticket (or any tracker).

## Why this exists (and why we don't upload to Jira automatically)

Per the project rule [`feedback_never_modify_jira.md`](../.claude-memory-link), modifications to Jira (ticket creation, comments, transitions) require explicit per-action user approval. **No tooling here writes to Jira.** Instead, on every test failure that the framework can classify, we produce a static `.md` file with all the context a human reviewer needs to:

1. Open the `.md` file
2. Read the auto-generated steps / actual / expected
3. Edit anything that doesn't match the real repro
4. Copy the contents into a Jira ticket manually

This way the human is always the gate between "test failed" and "ticket exists in tracker", and the file persists in source control as a reviewable artifact.

## How files appear here

The framework auto-writes a file when:

- A `BaseTest`-extending test fails
- `SmartBugDetector` classifies the failure (REAL_BUG / FLAKY_TEST / ENVIRONMENT_ISSUE / LOCATOR_CHANGE)
- `ReadyBugGenerator.generate()` is invoked from `@AfterMethod`

Optional enrichment:

- If the `CLAUDE_API_KEY` env var is set, `AIBugAnalyzer` calls Claude for a deeper root-cause + suggested fix and appends them to the file. Without the key, the file uses rules-based analysis only.

## File naming

```
ready-bug/<YYYY-MM-DD-HH-mm>-<TestClass>.<testMethod>.md
ready-bug/<YYYY-MM-DD-HH-mm>-<TestClass>.<testMethod>-screenshot.png
```

Files sort chronologically. The screenshot is copied next to the .md so a recipient who downloads the folder gets a self-contained package.

## File contents (template)

Every generated file follows this structure:

```markdown
# [Bug | High] AssetTestNG.testTC_Asset_07_EditAmpereRating

**Test:** `com.egalvanic.qa.testcase.AssetTestNG.testTC_Asset_07_EditAmpereRating`
**Date:** 2026-04-27T19:50:12
**Classification:** REAL_BUG (75% confidence)
**Severity:** HIGH
**Page URL:** https://acme.qa.egalvanic.ai/assets/<uuid>
**Page Title:** Egalvanic Platform
**Test Duration:** 27123 ms

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Login to `https://acme.qa.egalvanic.ai` with the standard QA credentials
2. Navigate to https://acme.qa.egalvanic.ai/assets/<uuid>
3. Open the existing record's Edit drawer/form
4. Modify the relevant field and save
5. Observe the result described under "Actual Result"

## Actual Result
StaleElementReferenceException: stale element reference: stale element not found...

## Expected Result
The test should complete without raising `StaleElementReferenceException`. See the test's
Javadoc / @Test description for the specific business intent.

## Screenshot
Attached: `testTC_Asset_07_EditAmpereRating_FAIL.png`

## Console Errors at Failure Time
[ERROR] /api/users/<uuid>/roles 401
[ERROR] Unhandled promise rejection: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined

## Likely Root Cause (rules-based)
StaleElementReference after MUI drawer re-render. The test cached a WebElement reference
across an edit drawer state transition; React replaced the underlying DOM node.

## Likely Root Cause (AI-enhanced, if Claude API key set)
[Claude's narrative root cause + 2-3 bullet fix recommendations]

---

_Auto-generated by `ReadyBugGenerator`. Review the above, edit if needed, then copy-paste
into your Jira ticket. **Do not** push this file's contents directly to Jira via tooling
— per project rule, Jira modifications need explicit per-ticket approval._
```

## Workflow for a tester

1. CI run completes; some tests failed
2. Browse to `ready-bug/` in the repo
3. Open each `.md` file
4. **Critical step:** verify the auto-generated steps actually reproduce the bug. AI heuristics can be wrong about user actions.
5. If steps look right → copy contents → paste into a new Jira ticket → attach the screenshot
6. If steps look wrong → either fix them in the .md file (so the next CI run picks up the corrected template) or write a separate Jira ticket from your manual investigation

## When to delete files from this folder

- **After a fix lands and is verified:** delete the file (or move to `ready-bug/archive/`)
- **When the bug is filed in Jira:** add a `> Filed as ZP-NNNN on YYYY-MM-DD` line at the top of the .md, but keep the file until verified-fixed
- **Never mass-delete:** each file is a piece of evidence; treat it like a screenshot

## Customizing the auto-generation

Source: [`src/main/java/com/egalvanic/qa/utils/ai/ReadyBugGenerator.java`](../src/main/java/com/egalvanic/qa/utils/ai/ReadyBugGenerator.java)

Key knobs:

- **Severity mapping** — see `severityFor()` in `ReadyBugGenerator`. Adjust the rules-to-severity table.
- **Step heuristics** — see `buildSteps()`. Currently switches on test method name keywords (create / delete / edit / login / auth). Add patterns for your domain's vocabulary.
- **AI enrichment threshold** — see `AIBugAnalyzer.analyze()`. Currently skips Claude when rules confidence ≥ 80%. Tune the threshold if you want to call Claude on every failure.

## Privacy / what's sent to Claude

When `AIBugAnalyzer` is enabled, the prompt sent to Claude includes:

- Test name
- Page URL (with credentials redacted)
- Exception type + message (with bearer tokens, JWTs, password=, token= patterns redacted)
- Console errors (truncated, redacted)
- Relevant DOM snippet (truncated)

What is NEVER sent:

- Plaintext passwords
- API tokens (JWTs are matched and redacted)
- Test user PII beyond the test email (which is already in `AppConstants`)

If your environment has stricter PII rules, set `CLAUDE_API_KEY=` (empty) to disable AI enrichment entirely. The framework will still generate `.md` files using rules-only analysis.
